data.title: Oil tanker linked to China made U-turn as ships test U.S. naval blockade of Iran - report
data.description:
A U.S.-sanctioned tanker linked to China that was being watched as one of the first vessels to test President Trump's naval blockade apparently has U-turned after sailing out of the Strait of Hormuz and into the Gulf of Oman, Bloomberg reported Tuesday.

The vessel Rich Starry was blacklisted by the U.S. in 2023 for helping Iran evade energy sanctions and has a history of spoofing its satellite signals, according to TankerTrackers.com, which monitors vessels using satellite data.

Another tanker, the Elpis, was in the strait and headed into the Gulf of Oman just as the blockade began; ship-tracking platforms Kpler and Vortexa indicate the vessel docked at an Iranian port in the gulf before attempting to pass through Hormuz, meaning it could be a potential target for the U.S. naval patrol.

The Elpis paused its journey at roughly the same location that Rich Starry turned around, although it has not broadcast a signal for several hours, meaning it could have carried on its journey or turned back.

China called the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports a "dangerous and irresponsible" move.

Benchmark crude oil futures fell sharply on Tuesday as signs of ​a possible resumption in talks to end the Iran war eased supply concerns stemming ‌from the blockade.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery down 5.9% to $93.23/bbl and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery down 4.4% to $88.86/bbl.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI)

data.title: Worksport CEO buys 88.2K shares, eyes $35–42M revenue in 2026
data.description:
• Worksport (WKSP) on Tuesday announced that CEO Steven Rossi bought 88.2K shares at $0.85, worth ~$75K, by converting his pending cash pay into stock—showing strong confidence in the company.
  • The buy comes amid rapid growth, with revenue increasing from ~$1.5M (2023) to $16.1M (2025) and aiming for $35–$42M in 2026.
  • Margins improved from ~11% to ~30%, targeting ~35% ahead in 2026.
  • Expansion is strong, as the dealer network expanded to 550+ locations, with ongoing product launches.
  • The company expects to turn cash-flow positive in 2026 at ~$9–$11M quarterly revenue.
  • Worksport (WKSP) is trading around ~$0.99, up ~4.7% today.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Ex-NATO chief warns U.K. is ‘under-prepared’ as defense spending row intensifies
data.description:
A senior figure in the U.K.’s Labor Party and former head of NATO has publicly rebuked Prime Minister Keir Starmer, arguing the government is not committing sufficient resources to national defense and is exposing the country to heightened risk, Reuters reported Tuesday.

George Robertson, who served as defense secretary in the late 1990s before leading NATO, said in remarks there is a disconnect between the prime minister’s language on security and actual spending decisions. He contended that the government has so far been unwilling to make the level of investment required.

Downing Street pushed back strongly. A spokesperson for the prime minister rejected the criticism outright, saying the government remains focused on ensuring that defense spending aligns with current and emerging threats.

The criticism also comes amid heightened scrutiny of Britain’s military posture overseas, following reports that a drone linked to Iran last month struck a U.K. airbase on Cyprus during a period of escalating hostilities between the United States and Israel and Iranian forces. The incident fueled concerns among defense observers about force protection and preparedness.

Robertson, who contributed to a Strategic Defense Review commissioned after Labor’s return to power in 2024, is expected to sharpen his critique in a speech later in Salisbury. Excerpts indicate he will argue that the U.K. has become increasingly vulnerable to external dangers.

In those prepared remarks, he is also set to single out Chancellor Rachel Reeves for giving minimal attention to defense in recent fiscal statements, including failing to address the issue in a subsequent update.

According to the excerpts, Robertson plans to warn that Britain is inadequately prepared for current threats, describing the country as exposed and insufficiently protected.

The government hasn’t yet released a long-term defense investment framework originally expected late last year, intended to implement recommendations from the 2024 review. That report emphasized the need to pivot toward emerging capabilities such as drone systems, cyber operations and data-driven warfare, drawing lessons from the conflict in Ukraine.

Starmer has argued that years of underfunding under previous Conservative governments created current shortfalls, and has pledged a significant increase in defense spending, targeting 3% of GDP in the next parliamentary term.

Still, Robertson is expected to accuse Britain’s leadership of complacency, criticizing what he characterizes as misguided fiscal decisions made by officials outside the defense establishment. He is also set to argue that rising welfare expenditures risk crowding out essential military investment.

The comments come as the government frames a more unstable global environment, highlighted by escalating tensions involving Iran, as justification for strengthening both economic resilience and military readiness.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Only two mid cap communication stocks have A grade EPS revisions
data.description:
As Q1 2026 earnings season gets underway, the communication services sector enters reporting season navigating a complex mix of tailwinds and headwinds, making upward EPS revisions a particularly valuable signal of where analyst conviction is building.

Within the mid-cap communication services space, that signal is notably rare: only two stocks have earned an "A" EPS revision grade from Seeking Alpha, reflecting the selective nature of analyst optimism in this segment of the market.

Array Digital Infrastructure (AD), a wireless telecommunications provider that has beaten earnings estimates in three of the last four quarters and Sphere Entertainment (SPHR), which delivered a stunning Q4 EPS turnaround. They stand alone as the mid-cap communication services names where analysts have been meaningfully revising their earnings expectations higher ahead of Q1 reports.

  • Array Digital Infrastructure, Inc. (AD) — EPS Revision Grade: A
  • Sphere Entertainment Co. (SPHR) — EPS Revision Grade: A

Communication Services ETFs: (XLC), (VOX), (IYZ), (RSPC), and (XTL)

data.title: Dollar share falls to 45%—are Bitcoin, gold the silent reserve rivals?
data.description:
The US dollar is still the world’s dominant reserve currency—but its supremacy is slowly weakening. And in that gap, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is starting to act less like a risky asset and more like a global backup plan.

According to Global Markets Investor, the  dollar’s share of global FX and gold reserves has fallen to ~45%, down from over ~60% at the start of the decade. IMF data also shows that, excluding gold (XAUUSD:CUR), the dollar now makes up ~57% of global reserves—the lowest level since 1994.

The direction of change is clear: central banks are slowly reducing overreliance on a single reserve asset. Three major reasons behind this shift are inflation volatility, shifting interest rate regimes, and rising geopolitical tensions.

Reportedly, global trade settled in dollars has dropped to ~40% from ~54% in just a few years, especially after the 2022 freezing of Russian reserves.

What is unfolding here is not just "de-dollarization," but instead a broader search for alternative stores of value. Among traditional assets, gold has emerged as the primary beneficiary of this diversification trend.

Central banks now hold more gold (XAUUSD:CUR) than US Treasuries (value-adjusted) for the first time, signaling a clear move toward politically neutral reserve assets, reported The Economic Times. However, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is no longer the only option being considered.

Most prominent companies have already started treating Bitcoin (BTC-USD) as a reserve asset. For example, Euronext-listed Capital B has increased its holdings to 2,925 BTC, showing a growing corporate view of BTC as a long-term treasury reserve asset.

Even entities like MicroStrategy (MSTR) continue to increase Bitcoin (BTC-USD) exposure. At the same time, the Bitcoin Policy Institute recently suggested that countries like Taiwan could use Bitcoin (BTC-USD) as a reserve asset to reduce dependence on the US dollar and protect against geopolitical risks.

What makes this more interesting is Bitcoin’s recent performance. Even during 46 days of US-Iran geopolitical tension, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has stayed strong.

It is currently trading near ~$75K, up around 10% in the past week and about 5% in the past month, despite continuous extreme fear sentiment across investors.

In conclusion, the US dollar is losing some of its dominance, but it’s not being replaced. Instead, the global money system is moving into a “coexistence era,” where multiple reserve assets exist together, each playing a role in providing stability, trust, and liquidity.

data.title: BlackRock signals at least $450M per quarter in share repurchases for 2026 while targeting low to mid-teens ACV growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BlackRock (BLK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Martin Small (Senior MD, CFO & Global Head of Corporate Strategy) said it was “a standout start to the year for BlackRock,” highlighting “$130 billion of net inflows” and “8% organic base fee growth,” while noting “we expanded margins by over 100 basis points.”
  • Small attributed growth to “deep engagement with clients,” adding that “BlackRock is winning mind share and wallet share,” and pointed to accelerating contributions from acquired and scaled platforms: “Aperio flows accelerating,” “iShares leading the industry,” and “infrastructure fundraising and deployment ahead of plan.”
  • Small framed the quarter against volatility, but contrasted client activity with sentiment: “While headlines and sentiment remain uneven, BlackRock's performance tells a very different story. Our fundamentals are strong.”
  • Small gave a capital return anchor: “we still anticipate repurchasing at least $450 million of shares per quarter for the balance of the year, consistent with our January guidance.”
  • Laurence Fink (Founder, CEO & Chairman) called Q1 “one of the strongest starts to a year in BlackRock's history,” tying momentum to platform breadth: “Clients awarded us with $130 billion of net inflows in the first quarter.”
  • Fink emphasized product and channel growth areas, including active ETFs: “Our active ETF platform has grown 4x in the last 2 years to more than $110 billion in AUM,” and reiterated a longer-term target: “we believe that active ETFs can be a $500 million or greater revenue generator by 2030, and we're already more than halfway there.”
  • Fink highlighted private markets integration: “The combinations of GIP and HPS with BlackRock are surpassing the highest expectations we underwrote,” and added on private credit: “Demand is structural.”

Outlook

  • Martin Small (Senior MD, CFO & Global Head of Corporate Strategy) did not provide formal EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks; instead, he reiterated a long-term technology target: “We remain committed to low to mid-teens ACV growth over the long term.”
  • Small reiterated the operating margin framework and suggested it is not capped at the current level: “we continue to target a 45% or greater adjusted operating margin,” and “I don't see 45% or 46% as a ceiling.”
  • Small linked near-term base fee sensitivity to markets: “At the end of March, our base fee entry rate was approximately 2% lower than the first quarter base fees, but that's basically been recovered with the April market performance.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from reflecting “12% organic base fee growth” in Q4 2025 (Small) to emphasizing durability and structural drivers in Q1 2026, with Small stating: “Organic growth is durable and broad-based.”

Financial Results

  • Martin Small (Senior MD, CFO & Global Head of Corporate Strategy) reported first quarter “revenue of $6.7 billion,” “operating income of $2.7 billion,” and “earnings per share of $12.53,” and said “our first quarter as-adjusted operating margin” was “44.5%.”
  • Small said results included acquisition impacts, stating revenue growth was driven by “the acquisitions of HPS and Preqin,” adding “approximately $230 million in base fees from HPS,” and that “Preqin added approximately $65 million to first quarter revenue.”
  • On below-the-line items, Small said “nonoperating results for the quarter included $66 million of net investment gains,” and “our as-adjusted tax rate for the first quarter was approximately 23%,” while reiterating: “We continue to estimate that 25% is a reasonable projected tax run rate for the remainder of 2026.”
  • On expenses and operating leverage, Small said “total expense increased 24% year-over-year,” while “excluding the impact of the HPS and Preqin acquisitions, G&A would have increased a mid-single-digit percentage from a year ago.”

Q&A

  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: asked about wealth channel penetration, alt milestones, and “the uptick in redemptions across evergreen private credit products”; Martin Small: said BlackRock manages “more than $1 trillion of assets for wealth managers,” cited “retail net inflows of $15 billion,” and added, “even with some moderation of private credit BDC flows, overall evergreen flows are pretty stable and steady,” while stating, “we're on track to bring an H series of vehicles to market for private wealth over the course of 2026.”
  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: asked about the Department of Labor proposal and target date funds with privates; Martin Small: said the DOL proposal “is better than we expected it to be,” and added, “We have our product coming to market with Great Gray this year. We're going to be launching a LifePath with privates.”
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked where BlackRock could gain share in dislocation and implications for organic growth; Martin Small: said BlackRock can “confidently and consistently deliver 6% to 7% growth from our structural growth segments,” and framed two drivers as “structural growers” and “whole portfolio relationships.”
  • Michael Brown, UBS: asked about sovereign wealth behavior amid Middle East conflict; Laurence Fink: said, “we have not seen any change in behavior,” and added, “We have an announcement that's forthcoming in the next week or so related to retirement win we have in the Middle East.”
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: asked about HPS trends, retail vs. institutional, and deployment; Martin Small: said HLEND “is one of the best-performing non-traded BDCs in the market,” reported it “logged 10.4% annualized total return since inception,” and stated, “85% to 90% of the base is institutional,” with institutional demand “strong.”
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO Capital Markets: asked about the filed NASDAQ-100 ETF (IQQ) and fee holiday; Martin Small: said, “we're not able to provide a lot of detail beyond what's in the filing,” but noted BlackRock manages “$25 billion” in NASDAQ-100 ETFs outside the U.S.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on potential pressure points (evergreen redemptions, private credit headlines, geopolitics) with questions like “what impact might we see from the uptick in redemptions” (Cyprys) and “geopolitical macro challenges” (Brown).
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, with confidence cues such as Fink’s “one of the strongest starts” and Small’s “we can confidently and consistently deliver 6% to 7% growth,” while also emphasizing limits on disclosure: “we're not able to provide a lot of detail” (Small on IQQ).
  • Versus Q4 2025, the current call carried more discussion of volatility and geopolitics (Fink: “real impactful geopolitical uncertainty”) while maintaining an upbeat posture around platform momentum.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized a shift toward international diversification and premium exposures, with Small citing “client demand for international iShares exposures,” while Q4 2025 focused more on year-end scale and record-setting flow context.
  • Margin messaging changed from Q4’s “45% operating margin” (Small) to Q1’s “44.5%” (Small) while keeping the same framework: “target a 45% or greater adjusted operating margin.”
  • Technology growth language moderated from Q4’s “ACV increased 31% year-over-year” (Small) to Q1’s “ACV, increased 14% year-over-year” (Small), while retaining the long-term “low to mid-teens ACV growth” commitment.
  • Analysts in Q1 leaned more into wealth-channel alternatives/evergreen and geopolitical questions, whereas Q4 analyst focus included money market sensitivity to rate cuts and deeper Preqin strategy discussions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Martin Small (Senior MD, CFO & Global Head of Corporate Strategy) cited market and geopolitical uncertainty: “Markets showed heightened sensitivity,” and “There is real impactful geopolitical uncertainty.”
  • Laurence Fink (Founder, CEO & Chairman) flagged uncertainty duration in the Middle East conflict: “things could change if there's a prolonged uncertainty and a prolonged violence in the region.”
  • On private credit activity levels, Fink noted: “New activity levels have been somewhat lower in the first quarter,” attributing it partly to seasonality and “market uncertainty,” while arguing opportunity improved with wider pricing: “direct lending opportunities are being quoted 25 to 50 basis points wider.”

Final Takeaway

BlackRock’s management described Q1 2026 as a historically strong start, anchored in $130B of net inflows, 8% organic base fee growth, and continued margin expansion, while underscoring that clients are consolidating with scaled platforms across ETFs, tax-aware SMAs, private markets, and Aladdin. In Q&A, executives positioned evergreen and private-credit access for wealth as steady despite some moderation, reiterated retirement-with-privates momentum tied to the DOL proposal and planned LifePath launches, and indicated sovereign wealth behavior has not changed amid Middle East conflict, alongside an upcoming retirement-related regional announcement. Capital return plans remained consistent with at least $450M of share repurchases per quarter for the rest of 2026, and management maintained long-term targets around low-to-mid-teens technology ACV growth and a 45%+ adjusted operating margin framework.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. equity overvaluation fears hit 2019 low - BofA
data.description:
Investor anxiety over U.S. equity valuations has dropped to its lowest level since February 2019, according to Bank of America’s April Global Fund Manager Survey released on Tuesday, signaling a shift in market sentiment after years of persistent concerns about overpriced stocks.

The survey showed a net 64% of respondents now consider U.S. stocks overvalued—a reading that, while still elevated, marks a notable retreat from the extreme pessimism that had dominated fund manager outlooks in recent years. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) is fully recovering losses tied to the war in Iran and sitting just ~1% below its all-time high of just over 7,000 recorded on January 28.

The shift indicates that some of the excess valuation anxiety built up over the past several years has begun to dissipate in the face of the Middle East conflict and higher-for-longer borrowing costs. For much of this economic cycle, U.S. equities were seen as crowded, expensive and difficult to justify on valuation grounds amid the AI boom.

data.title: Rent the Runway projects Q1 revenue of $85M-$87M while targeting FY2026 rental products acquired of $45M-$50M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rent the Runway (RENT) Q4 fiscal 2025

Management View

  • Jennifer Hyman (Co-Founder, CEO, President & Director) framed fiscal 2025 as a proof point for a larger inventory strategy, saying, "One year ago, we announced that we were making our biggest inventory investment in Rent the Runway history to drive growth" and adding, "Today, I am proud to report that this strategy has been successful." She reported the company "grew our active subscriber base by 20%, ending the year with 144,000 subscribers" and tied engagement gains to inventory breadth, noting, "Inventory-related cancellations dropped 7.6% year-over-year in Q4" and "our average subscriber visits our app 15 times per month."

  • Jennifer Hyman (CEO) described a strategic shift in fiscal 2026 from acquiring inventory to improving how customers find it, stating, "If 2025 was about inventory acquisition, 2026 is about discovery." She outlined AI-driven front-end initiatives including "outfit groupings," a more dynamic product detail page experience, and a vision for "a state-of-the-art conversational agent" to help customers search by occasion.

  • Jennifer Hyman (CEO) also laid out new revenue initiatives beyond core subscriptions, including "piloting an online marketplace, launching B2B dry cleaning services, expanding our advertising revenue program and more." She said, "In March, we launched a pilot of our Rent the Runway marketplace" and cited survey results that "86% of members surveyed are interested in purchasing these complementary items from us."

  • Siddharth Thacker (Chief Financial Officer) connected the year’s growth effort to cash usage, stating, "free cash flow declining to negative $46 million in fiscal year 2025 from negative $7.2 million in fiscal year 2024" and attributing it primarily to the decision "to front-load inventory investments." He added, "we don't anticipate significant increases in new inventory receipts in fiscal year 2026" and said the company expects "improved free cash flow in fiscal year 2026."

Outlook

  • Siddharth Thacker (Chief Financial Officer) guided Q1 revenue to "between $85 million and $87 million" and said the sequential decline from Q4 is "primarily expected to be driven by lower resale revenue in Q1 '26." He guided Q1 adjusted EBITDA margins to "between negative 5% and negative 7% of revenue" and attributed the year-over-year margin pressure to "significantly higher revenue share expenses" from a "much larger proportion of inventory receipts" coming through revenue share.

  • Siddharth Thacker (CFO) guided fiscal 2026 to "double-digit growth in revenue versus fiscal year 2025" while flagging tougher comparisons later in the year because Q3 fiscal 2025 and beyond included the price increase impact. He also guided fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA to "between 4% and 7% of revenue" and said the mix shift toward revenue share inventory is expected to push "revenue share expenses as a percentage of revenue" higher versus fiscal 2025.

Financial Results

  • Siddharth Thacker (Chief Financial Officer) reported Q4 revenue of "$91.7 million" and said it was "up $15.3 million or 20% year-over-year" and "up $4.1 million or 4.7% quarter-over-quarter." He reported ending active subscribers of "143,796" and noted the sequential decline from Q3 was "primarily due to seasonal factors."

  • Siddharth Thacker (CFO) highlighted margin and cost movements, reporting fulfillment costs of "$21.6 million" and fulfillment costs as a percentage of revenue of "23.6%"; he said carrier rate increases and higher warehouse processing costs were offsets. He reported Q4 gross margin of "38.6%" and said revenue share costs were higher as a percentage of revenue due to greater Share by RTR levels.

  • Siddharth Thacker (CFO) reported Q4 adjusted EBITDA of "$18.3 million" and noted it included a benefit as "adjusted EBITDA margins for Q4 '25 were positively impacted by 2.1% due to the reversal of incentive compensation accruals." He reported Q4 free cash flow of "$0.5 million" and reiterated full-year free cash flow of "negative $46 million" tied to inventory purchases.

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • The absence of Q&A removed analyst tone signals in the current call; the transcript contained only prepared remarks and did not include analyst questions.

  • Management tone was positive in describing subscriber growth, engagement, and new initiatives; Jennifer Hyman (Co-Founder, CEO, President & Director) said, "I firmly believe that Rent the Runway is in the strongest position in years," while Siddharth Thacker (Chief Financial Officer) emphasized cautious drivers of FY2026 profitability, including "significantly higher revenue share expenses" and an "uncertain" macro and geopolitical environment.

  • Versus the prior quarter, the prepared-remarks tone remained confident around growth and the recapitalization, but shifted from highlighting the completion of the recapitalization to emphasizing execution risk and economics around revenue share mix, with Siddharth Thacker (CFO) adding that guidance "does not contemplate material deterioration or volatility" in factors like transportation costs and consumer confidence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Compared with Q3, the current call expanded strategic emphasis from inventory acquisition and product improvements into a broader 2026 agenda that Jennifer Hyman (Co-Founder, CEO, President & Director) summarized as "2026 is about discovery" and added revenue diversification pillars including marketplace, advertising partnerships, and B2B services.

  • Financially, Q4 showed higher revenue and materially higher gross margin versus Q3 (with management attributing Q4 margin improvement to seasonality, price increase run-rate, and revenue share fixed-cost dynamics), while fiscal 2026 guidance introduced an explicit expectation of lower rental product capital spending, with rental products acquired guided to "between $45 million and $50 million" versus "$74.9 million" in fiscal 2025.

  • On investor-relevant risks, both quarters discussed revenue share expense pressure; the current call put greater emphasis on the FY2026 mix impact to adjusted EBITDA margins and explicitly warned of uncertainty in transportation costs, fuel surcharges, and consumer confidence.

Risks and Concerns

  • Siddharth Thacker (Chief Financial Officer) said Q1 margin pressure is expected to come from "significantly higher revenue share expenses" driven by both higher fixed payments and variable expenses as the base of revenue share inventory grows.

  • Siddharth Thacker (CFO) cautioned that "the macroeconomic and geopolitical environment remains highly uncertain" with potential impacts including "transportation costs, fuel surcharges and consumer confidence" and said results could differ materially if conditions change.

Final Takeaway

Management presented fiscal 2025 as a return to subscriber and revenue growth driven by a step-up in inventory, alongside a recapitalization that reduced total debt from approximately $319 million to $120 million. For fiscal 2026, leadership emphasized a shift toward AI-driven discovery, community-led marketing, and new revenue streams (marketplace, advertising partnerships, and B2B dry cleaning), while the CFO guided to Q1 revenue of $85 million to $87 million and Q1 adjusted EBITDA margins of negative 5% to negative 7%, citing higher revenue share costs. For the full year, management guided to double-digit revenue growth, adjusted EBITDA of 4% to 7% of revenue, and rental products acquired of $45 million to $50 million, while flagging ongoing uncertainty in transportation costs and consumer confidence.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Astronova expects mid-single-digit fiscal 2027 revenue growth as $2M annualized royalty benefit begins in Q4
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AstroNova, Inc. (ALOT) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • "The second half of fiscal 2026 was a reset period for AstroNova, and our results reflect the early benefits of the changes we have made across the business." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • "We entered the year with a focus on stabilizing the company, improving cash generation, reducing debt and raising accountability across both segments, and we delivered against those priorities." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • "As you know, we announced that the Board is evaluating a range of potential strategic alternatives, which may include, among other things, a sale of all or part of the company, a strategic investment, a merger or other business combination, other strategic or financial options or continuing to execute on our organic strategic plan." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann) "We are early in the process, and as you would expect, we cannot speculate on the outcome." (President, CEO & Director Ittmann)
  • "Product ID orders were $27.5 million, up $2.9 million year-over-year, resulting in a book-to-bill ratio of 104% and backlog increased by $1.1 million sequentially as our new go-to-market strategy continued to gain traction." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • "Orders in Aerospace were $13.6 million, resulting in a book-to-bill ratio of 122% and year-end backlog was $12 million." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann) "ToughWriter now represents more than 80% of total flight deck printers shipments." (President, CEO & Director Ittmann)
  • "Fourth quarter revenue was $37.5 million, up $0.2 million compared with the prior year period." (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle) "Tariff mitigation actions contributed approximately $0.6 million to revenue in the quarter, and the foreign currency translation provided a $0.8 million benefit." (VP, Treasurer & CFO DeByle)

Outlook

  • "Looking ahead, for fiscal 2027, we expect mid-single-digit revenue growth and expansion in adjusted EBITDA margin." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • "In Aerospace, we anticipated measured top line growth supported by rising aircraft utilization and favorable shift in product mix and the expiration of a major royalty obligation in the third quarter of fiscal 2027, which will provide an approximate $2 million annualized contribution to gross profit beginning in the fourth quarter." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s full-year framing, Q4 shifted to a fiscal 2027 setup and emphasized that "we have spent the last 6 months positioning AstroNova for improved and more sustainable performance" (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann), alongside the strategic alternatives review.

Financial results

  • "Net loss for the quarter was $1.1 million or $0.15 per diluted share." (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle) "Non-GAAP net loss was $0.3 million or $0.04 per share." (VP, Treasurer & CFO DeByle)
  • "Gross profit for the fourth quarter was $11.3 million and gross margin was 30.2%, reflecting a contraction of 250 to 260 basis points year-over-year, primarily to lower volume and mix." (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle) "On a non-GAAP basis, gross profit was $11.9 million and non-GAAP gross margin was 31.7%." (VP, Treasurer & CFO DeByle)
  • "Adjusted EBITDA in the fourth quarter grew 18% to $3.3 million, while adjusted EBITDA margin expanded 130 basis points to 8.8%." (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle) "For the full fiscal year 2026, adjusted EBITDA was $12.7 million." (VP, Treasurer & CFO DeByle)
  • "Cash provided by operating activities in the fourth quarter was $3.7 million" and "cash from operations was $11.7 million" for the full year. (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle) "Capital expenditures were tightly controlled at $0.3 million for the year." (VP, Treasurer & CFO DeByle)
  • "During the fourth quarter, we reduced debt by $2.7 million, bringing total debt to $37.6 million." (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle) "We ended the year with $4.1 million of cash and cash equivalents and total liquidity of $15.9 million." (VP, Treasurer & CFO DeByle)

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript ("There are no questions at this time."). (Operator)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: No questions were asked in the current quarter, limiting direct read-through on analyst skepticism or focus areas.
  • Management: Slightly positive in tone, anchored in execution claims and forward-looking margin drivers, including "we delivered against those priorities" and "we expect mid-single-digit revenue growth." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • Versus the prior quarter: Both calls had no questions, while Q4 introduced a clearer potential catalyst and uncertainty with "the Board is evaluating a range of potential strategic alternatives." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategic focus: Q4 added a board-led strategic alternatives process; Q3 centered on an operating transformation narrative and reiterated fiscal 2026 guidance.
  • Guidance language: Q3 said, "We are reiterating our guidance for the full year of fiscal 2026" and provided a revenue range and adjusted EBITDA margin range; Q4 moved to fiscal 2027 targets of "mid-single-digit revenue growth" and "expansion in adjusted EBITDA margin." (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • Key business drivers: Both quarters emphasized ToughWriter mix at "more than 80%" of flight deck shipments and a fiscal 2027 royalty roll-off; Q4 quantified the roll-off as "approximately a $2 million annualized benefit to gross profit" beginning in fiscal Q4 2027. (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)

Risks and concerns

  • "We are early in the process, and as you would expect, we cannot speculate on the outcome" of strategic alternatives, leaving timing and result unspecified. (President, CEO & Director Jorik Ittmann)
  • "Gross margin" faced pressure "primarily to lower volume and mix" in Q4, with management positioning second-half comparisons as the better indicator of trajectory. (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle)
  • "Quarter-to-quarter order patterns do not necessarily reflect underlying demand" given Aerospace project timing, which management highlighted as a recurring variability factor. (VP, Treasurer & CFO Thomas DeByle)

Final takeaway

Management framed fiscal 2026 as a second-half “reset,” pointed to improving orders and backlog visibility, and guided to mid-single-digit fiscal 2027 revenue growth with adjusted EBITDA margin expansion. The call elevated two stock-relevant swing factors: the board’s active review of strategic alternatives and a royalty expiration expected to contribute about $2 million annualized to gross profit beginning in fiscal Q4 2027, while the absence of analyst questions left limited external probing of execution and timing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BNP Paribas starts coverage of HVAC sector: Near-term chill amid long-term growth
data.description:
The U.S. HVAC industry is entering a period of mixed performance, with strong long-term growth drivers offset by near-term weakness in residential markets, according to a new research report from BNP Paribas.

BNP Paribas U.S. HVAC  Coverage
       
Company (ticker) Rating
Trane Technologies (TT) Outperform
Vertiv (VRT) Outperform
Lennox International (LII) Neutral
Carrier Global (CARR) Neutral
Johnson Controls (JCI)  Underperform

The sector, which generates about $230 billion in annual global revenue, is being reshaped by structural forces such as electrification, energy efficiency mandates, digitization and the rapid expansion of AI-driven data centers. These trends are expected to support above-average growth over time, even as short-term conditions remain uneven.

Data centers drive next growth phase

Analysts led by Andrew Buscaglia highlight data centers as the most important driver of incremental demand, particularly as artificial intelligence workloads increase cooling requirements.

Companies focused on thermal management and liquid cooling are positioned to benefit most. Vertiv (VRT), which derives more than 80% of its revenue from data centers, is seen as a direct beneficiary of rising demand for high-density cooling systems.

Trane Technologies (TT) is also expected to gain share, with analysts pointing to its pricing power and exposure to commercial HVAC markets. Both companies were initiated with positive ratings.

“Data centers will drive above-average growth in HVAC this cycle,” the report said, with demand for advanced cooling solutions accelerating as AI infrastructure expands.

Residential market remains a drag

In contrast, residential HVAC continues to face headwinds. Higher interest rates and housing affordability challenges are limiting new construction and slowing demand for new systems.

The report expects weakness in residential markets to persist through at least 2026, with a more meaningful recovery unlikely until 2027.

Recent regulatory changes tied to refrigerant transitions have also disrupted volumes, though those effects are beginning to ease. Even so, analysts warn that pricing fatigue among consumers could weigh on demand in the near term.

Commercial outlook mixed outside of AI

Outside of data centers, commercial HVAC demand remains uneven.

Segments such as healthcare and education are showing resilience, while office and retail markets continue to face uncertainty. Manufacturing demand is expected to improve following delayed capital spending.

Overall, analysts describe the commercial outlook as mixed, with data centers standing out as the clearest source of growth.

Stock picks reflect diverging exposure

BNP Paribas initiated coverage on five HVAC-related companies with varying ratings based on their exposure to these trends.

  • Trane Technologies (TT) and Vertiv (VRT) received positive ratings
  • Carrier Global (CARR) and Lennox International (LII) were rated neutral
  • Johnson Controls (JCI) was assigned a negative rating

The firm cited structural advantages and data center exposure as key differentiators, while warning that companies tied more heavily to residential markets face ongoing pressure.

Valuations reflect AI optimism

Valuations across the sector have risen in recent quarters, driven in part by investor enthusiasm for AI infrastructure.

Data center–focused companies command higher multiples, while traditional HVAC names trade at a discount, especially those with greater residential exposure.

Despite elevated valuations, analysts note that the sector is now trading slightly below the broader industrials index, which could present opportunities if fundamentals improve.

Long-term outlook remains intact

Over the longer term, the report remains constructive on HVAC, citing durable demand tied to energy efficiency and climate trends.

Buildings account for roughly 40% of global energy consumption, and HVAC systems play a central role in reducing emissions and improving efficiency.

At the same time, only about 10% of people living in the world’s hottest climates have access to air conditioning, underscoring the potential for future growth.

While near-term conditions may remain uneven, analysts conclude that structural drivers are likely to support sustained expansion across the industry over the coming decade.
